contaminant.name: Surface Radioactive Contamination
contaminant.description: Radioactive contamination, it consists of low-level particle deposits arising from nuclear processes or environmental exposure, which manifest as adherent layers that emit alpha or beta radiation, distinguishing themselves from chemical contaminants through their persistent isotopic signatures and minimal visibility under standard inspection. On materials such as stainless steel or concrete, these deposits behave tenaciously, embedding into porous surfaces or forming oxide bonds on metals like copper and titanium, influenced from surface roughness that complicates uniform adhesion. Key removal challenges include avoiding secondary dispersion of particles, yet laser cleaning proves effective, as the pulsed energy vaporizes the thin layers selectively without mechanical abrasion, preserving the underlying substrate integrity.

fireExplosionRisk.description: Minimal fire risk with standard precautions and adequate ventilation
fireExplosionRisk.mitigation: Standard fire safety precautions, extinguisher available within 15m

toxicGasRisk.description: Radioactive particulates (resuspended) generation detected - low toxicity risk
toxicGasRisk.mitigation: N95 or P100 respirator for particulate control, standard ventilation. WARNING: Radioactive particulates (resuspended) - known carcinogen(s), minimize exposure

visibilityHazard.description: Moderate visibility reduction (40-60%), significant particulate haze
visibilityHazard.mitigation: Ensure clear sight lines, use source extraction, maintain awareness of surroundings

ventilationRequirements.rationale: Standard industrial ventilation (12 ACH) for particulate control
